mission.title: Install and Configure Cluster API (CAPI)
mission.description: Cluster API (CAPI) provides declarative APIs and tooling to simplify the provisioning, upgrading, and operation of multiple Kubernetes clusters. It is particularly useful for platform operators looking for consistent and repeatable cluster deployments across various infrastructure environments. Key trade-offs include the complexity of setup and the need for familiarity with Kubernetes concepts.

steps.title: Add the Cluster API Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Cluster API Helm repository to your local Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add cluster-api https://charts.cluster-api.sigs.k8s.io

```

> This repository contains the necessary charts for installation.

steps.title: Install the Cluster API Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are required for Cluster API to function properly, as they define the custom resources used by the operator.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-components.yaml

```

> This command installs the necessary CRDs.

steps.title: Install the Cluster API controller
steps.description:
This step installs the Cluster API controller using Helm, which manages the lifecycle of clusters.

```bash

helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version 1.12.4

```

> Ensure you are installing in the kube-system namespace.

steps.title: Create a Cluster API custom resource
steps.description:
This step creates a custom resource that defines a new Kubernetes cluster to be managed by Cluster API.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-infrastructure
EOF

```

> Replace 'MyInfrastructureProviderCluster' with your specific infrastructure provider.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-infrastructure
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Cluster API installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Cluster API components are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> You should see the Cluster API controller pods running.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Cluster API
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Cluster API components and CRDs. Be aware that this will lead to the loss of all managed clusters and their resources.

```bash

helm uninstall cluster-api --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-components.yaml

```

> This command removes the Cluster API controller.
> This command removes the CRDs.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Cluster API
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your cluster configurations. This step upgrades the Cluster API controller to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get clusters -o yaml > clusters-backup.yaml

helm upgrade cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version 1.12.4

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> Backup existing cluster configurations.
> This command upgrades the Cluster API controller.
> Verify that the pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'no matches for kind "Cluster" in version "cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1"'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Cluster CRD has not been installed or is not available.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRDs using the command: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-components.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'failed to connect to the API server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This typically indicates that the Kubernetes API server is down or unreachable.

**Fix:**
Check the status of your Kubernetes cluster and ensure that the API server is running.

troubleshooting.title: Pods in kube-system are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to misconfiguration or missing dependencies.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the pods using: kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kube-system to identify the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'unable to recognize "": no matches for kind "MyInfrastructureProviderCluster"'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the specific infrastructure provider CRD is not installed.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the CRD for your infrastructure provider is installed and available.

versionNotes.changes: Fixed issues with cluster scaling and improved error messages for better debugging.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get clusters' showing your created clusters with their statuses.

EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
